expanded_description: A comprehensive introduction to chemical reactions, exploring how substances transform into new substances through the breaking and forming of chemical bonds, the evidence that indicates a reaction has occurred, and the fundamental principles that govern all chemical changes.

core_concept.principle: Chemical reactions involve the rearrangement of atoms through the breaking of existing chemical bonds and the formation of new chemical bonds, resulting in substances with different properties than the original reactants.
core_concept.explanation: During a chemical reaction, atoms are neither created nor destroyed (Law of Conservation of Mass); they are simply rearranged into new combinations. Reactants are the starting substances that undergo change, while products are the new substances formed. Chemical reactions are represented by chemical equations that show reactants on the left, products on the right, and an arrow indicating the direction of change. Evidence of chemical reactions includes color changes, gas production, precipitate formation, temperature changes, and light emission. Understanding chemical reactions is fundamental to all chemistry because it explains how matter transforms and how new substances are created.

image.alt: Diagram showing reactants transforming into products through bond breaking and forming
image.caption: Chemical reactions: atoms rearrange through bond breaking and forming; atoms are conserved but substances change.

sections.introduction: Chemical reactions occur constantly around us—from digestion to combustion to photosynthesis. While we can't see atoms rearranging, we can observe evidence that indicates a chemical reaction has occurred. Recognizing these signs helps distinguish chemical changes from physical changes.

key_concept.definition: Evidence of chemical reactions includes observable changes that indicate new substances have formed: color change, gas production (bubbling), precipitate formation (solid from solution), temperature change (heat absorbed or released), and light emission.
key_concept.context: These indicators suggest chemical bonds have broken and formed, creating new substances. However, some physical changes can mimic these signs, so multiple pieces of evidence strengthen the conclusion that a chemical reaction occurred.

classifications.value: Observable change in color indicating new substance formation
classifications.behavior: Color changes occur when new substances have different light absorption properties than reactants, often due to changes in electron configuration or oxidation states.

examples.explanation: Silver-gray iron ($\text{Fe}$) reacts with oxygen to form reddish-brown rust ($\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$). The color change indicates iron has been oxidized to a new compound with different properties.

image.alt: Iron nail rusting showing color change from silver to brown
image.caption: Iron rusting: color change from silver-gray to reddish-brown indicates chemical reaction.

examples.explanation: Shiny copper ($\text{Cu}$) turns green when exposed to air, forming copper carbonate ($\text{CuCO}_3$). The Statue of Liberty's green color comes from this chemical reaction.

image.alt: Copper turning green from oxidation
image.caption: Copper oxidation: shiny brown copper turns green, forming new compound.

classifications.value: Formation of gas bubbles indicating new gaseous product
characteristics: Gas may be visible or invisible
classifications.behavior: Gas production occurs when a reaction produces a gaseous product that escapes from solution, visible as bubbles. Common gases include $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{H}_2$, $\text{O}_2$, and $\text{NH}_3$.

examples.explanation: $\text{NaHCO}_3 + \text{CH}_3\text{COOH} \rightarrow \text{NaCH}_3\text{COO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2\uparrow$. Carbon dioxide gas bubbles form, indicating chemical reaction. Used in volcanoes and baking.

image.alt: Baking soda and vinegar reaction producing bubbles
image.caption: Baking soda + vinegar: $\text{CO}_2$ gas bubbles indicate chemical reaction.

examples.explanation: $\text{Zn} + 2\text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{ZnCl}_2 + \text{H}_2\uparrow$. Zinc reacts with hydrochloric acid to produce hydrogen gas bubbles. Test with burning splint confirms $\text{H}_2$.

image.alt: Zinc reacting with acid producing hydrogen gas
image.caption: Metal + acid: hydrogen gas bubbles indicate chemical reaction.

classifications.value: Solid forming from solution indicating insoluble product
classifications.behavior: Precipitates form when two aqueous solutions react to produce an insoluble solid that cannot remain dissolved. The solid particles make the solution cloudy before settling.

examples.explanation: $\text{AgNO}_3(aq) + \text{NaCl}(aq) \rightarrow \text{AgCl}(s)\downarrow + \text{NaNO}_3(aq)$. White solid ($\text{AgCl}$) precipitates from clear solutions, indicating chemical reaction.

image.alt: White precipitate forming from two clear solutions
image.caption: Precipitate formation: clear solutions become cloudy with solid $\text{AgCl}$.

examples.explanation: $\text{Pb}(\text{NO}_3)_2(aq) + 2\text{KI}(aq) \rightarrow \text{PbI}_2(s)\downarrow + 2\text{KNO}_3(aq)$. Bright yellow $\text{PbI}_2$ precipitate forms, dramatic color change indicates reaction.

image.alt: Yellow lead iodide precipitate
image.caption: Lead iodide: bright yellow precipitate from colorless solutions.

classifications.value: Heat absorbed or released indicating energy change
characteristics: May occur without other visible signs
classifications.behavior: Temperature changes occur because breaking bonds requires energy (endothermic) while forming bonds releases energy (exothermic). Net temperature change depends on which process dominates.

examples.explanation: $\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{heat}$. Burning methane releases heat and light. Container becomes hot. Exothermic reactions feel warm.

image.alt: Methane combustion releasing heat and light
image.caption: Combustion: exothermic reaction releases heat and light.

examples.explanation: $\text{NH}_4\text{NO}_3(s) + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{NH}_4^+(aq) + \text{NO}_3^-(aq) - \text{heat}$. Dissolving ammonium nitrate absorbs heat. Container becomes cold. Endothermic reactions feel cool.

image.alt: Chemical cold pack becoming cold
image.caption: Cold pack: endothermic reaction absorbs heat, feels cold.

classifications.behavior: Light emission occurs when electrons in newly formed molecules drop to lower energy states, releasing photons. Common in combustion, glow sticks, and bioluminescence.

examples.explanation: Chemical reaction between hydrogen peroxide and phenyl oxalate ester produces light without heat. Bending stick mixes chemicals, reaction produces glow. Light indicates chemical change.

image.alt: Glow stick emitting light from chemical reaction
image.caption: Glow stick: chemiluminescence produces light from chemical reaction.

examples.explanation: $2\text{Mg} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{MgO} + \text{light} + \text{heat}$. Magnesium ribbon burns with brilliant white light. Intense light indicates vigorous chemical reaction.

image.alt: Magnesium ribbon burning with bright white light
image.caption: Magnesium combustion: brilliant white light indicates chemical reaction.

principles.statement: Observing multiple types of evidence (color change + gas + heat) provides stronger evidence of chemical reaction than a single indicator alone.
principles.application: Distinguishes chemical changes from physical changes; some physical changes mimic single indicators (ice melting feels cold but is physical).
principles.example: Burning wood: color change (black ash) + gas (smoke) + heat (warmth) + light (flame) = definite chemical reaction. Ice melting: feels cold but only physical change.
principles.analogy: Like detective work: one clue might be misleading; multiple clues confirm the conclusion.

image.alt: Diagram showing multiple pieces of evidence for chemical reaction
image.caption: Multiple evidence: color + gas + heat + light confirms chemical reaction.

principles.statement: Physical changes alter appearance or state without changing chemical composition; chemical changes produce new substances with different properties.
principles.application: Melting ice (physical): $\text{H}_2\text{O}(s) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$, same substance. Burning wood (chemical): wood $\rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{ash}$, new substances.
principles.example: Physical: tearing paper, melting ice, dissolving sugar. Chemical: burning paper, rusting iron, digesting food. Chemical changes are usually irreversible.

image.alt: Comparison of physical and chemical changes
image.caption: Physical change: same substance, different form. Chemical change: new substances formed.

components.equation: $\text{Chemical Reaction Evidence} = \begin{cases} \text{Color change} \\ \text{Gas production} \\ \text{Precipitate formation} \\ \text{Temperature change} \\ \text{Light emission} \end{cases}$
components.interpretation: One or more indicators suggest chemical reaction; multiple indicators provide stronger evidence. Always consider if change is physical or chemical.

worked_examples.title: Identifying Evidence of Chemical Reactions
worked_examples.problem: For each observation, determine if it indicates a chemical reaction or physical change. Explain your reasoning: (a) Ice melting to water, (b) Iron nail rusting, (c) Sugar dissolving in water, (d) Baking soda and vinegar bubbling, (e) Glass breaking.

step_by_step.calculation: Ice melting: $\text{H}_2\text{O}(s) \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$. Same substance ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$), only state changes. No new substance = physical change.
Iron rusting: $\text{Fe} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$. Color change (silver to brown), new substance formed. Chemical reaction.
Sugar dissolving: $\text{C}_{12}\text{H}_{22}\text{O}_{11}(s) \rightarrow \text{C}_{12}\text{H}_{22}\text{O}_{11}(aq)$. Same substance, can recover by evaporation. Physical change.
$\text{NaHCO}_3 + \text{CH}_3\text{COOH} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2\uparrow + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{NaCH}_3\text{COO}$. Gas bubbles, new substances. Chemical reaction.
Glass breaking: only size/shape changes. Still glass ($\text{SiO}_2$). No new substance. Physical change.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Physical change (same substance, state change). (b) Chemical reaction (color change, new substance $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$). (c) Physical change (same substance, can recover). (d) Chemical reaction (gas production, new substances). (e) Physical change (only size changes). Key: chemical changes produce new substances; physical changes do not.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Distinguishing chemical reactions from physical changes using evidence indicators

worked_examples.title: Analyzing Multiple Evidence for Chemical Reactions
worked_examples.problem: A student mixes two clear, colorless solutions. The mixture becomes warm, turns yellow, and a yellow solid settles at the bottom. Identify all evidence of chemical reaction and explain why this indicates a chemical change.

step_by_step.calculation: Observations: (1) Two clear solutions mixed, (2) Mixture becomes warm, (3) Turns yellow, (4) Yellow solid forms
Warm = temperature change (exothermic). Yellow color = color change. Yellow solid = precipitate formation. Three types of evidence!
Multiple evidence types (temperature + color + precipitate) = strong evidence of chemical reaction. New substance (yellow solid) formed.

step_by_step.explanation: Explain why this indicates chemical change.
step_by_step.calculation: New substance with different properties (yellow solid from clear solutions) formed. Energy released (warmth). Cannot easily reverse. Definite chemical reaction.

worked_examples.final_answer: Evidence observed: (1) Temperature change (warmth = exothermic), (2) Color change (clear to yellow), (3) Precipitate formation (yellow solid). Three types of evidence provide strong confirmation of chemical reaction. New substance formed with different properties than reactants. This is a chemical change, not physical.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Using multiple pieces of evidence to confirm chemical reaction occurred

sections.introduction: Chemical equations are the language chemists use to describe chemical reactions. They show what substances react (reactants), what substances form (products), and in what proportions. Learning to read and write chemical equations is essential for understanding and predicting chemical reactions.

key_concept.definition: A chemical equation is a symbolic representation of a chemical reaction showing reactants on the left, products on the right, and an arrow indicating the direction of change. Coefficients indicate the relative amounts of each substance.
key_concept.context: Chemical equations must be balanced to satisfy the Law of Conservation of Mass—atoms are neither created nor destroyed in chemical reactions. The number of each type of atom must be equal on both sides of the equation.

classifications.value: Substances before and after chemical reaction
characteristics: Arrow points from reactants to products
classifications.behavior: Reactants are consumed during the reaction; products are formed. The arrow indicates the direction of change (reactants $\rightarrow$ products).

examples.explanation: $\text{Reactants} \rightarrow \text{Products}$. Example: $2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$. $\text{H}_2$ and $\text{O}_2$ are reactants; $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ is product. Arrow shows direction.

image.alt: Diagram showing reactants on left, products on right of arrow
image.caption: Chemical equation: reactants $\rightarrow$ products.

examples.explanation: $\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$. Two reactants ($\text{CH}_4$, $\text{O}_2$), two products ($\text{CO}_2$, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$). Plus signs separate multiple substances.

image.alt: Equation with multiple reactants and products
image.caption: Multiple reactants and products separated by + signs.

classifications.behavior: Coefficients indicate how many molecules/moles; subscripts indicate atoms per molecule. Changing subscripts changes the substance; changing coefficients changes the amount.

examples.explanation: $2\text{H}_2\text{O}$: coefficient 2 means 2 water molecules. Total: 4 H atoms (2 × 2) and 2 O atoms (2 × 1). Coefficient multiplies entire formula.

image.alt: Diagram showing coefficient multiplies entire formula
image.caption: Coefficient 2: means 2 molecules of $\text{H}_2\text{O}$.

examples.explanation: $\text{H}_2\text{O}$: subscript 2 means 2 H atoms per water molecule. Cannot change to $\text{H}_3\text{O}$ (different substance). Subscripts define the substance identity.

image.alt: Diagram showing subscripts indicate atoms per molecule
image.caption: Subscript 2: means 2 H atoms per $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ molecule.

classifications.value: Indicators of physical state of substances
classifications.behavior: State symbols provide additional information about the physical state of each substance. Important for understanding reaction conditions and predicting products.

examples.explanation: $2\text{H}_2(g) + \text{O}_2(g) \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$. Reactants are gases (g), product is liquid (l). State symbols clarify physical states during reaction.

image.alt: Chemical equation with state symbols
image.caption: State symbols: (s) solid, (l) liquid, (g) gas, (aq) aqueous.

examples.explanation: $\text{AgNO}_3(aq) + \text{NaCl}(aq) \rightarrow \text{AgCl}(s)\downarrow + \text{NaNO}_3(aq)$. Aqueous reactants form solid precipitate. (aq) = dissolved, (s) = solid precipitate.

image.alt: Precipitation reaction with state symbols
image.caption: State symbols show aqueous solutions forming solid precipitate.

classifications.behavior: Reaction conditions written above or below the arrow indicate special requirements. Catalysts speed up reactions without being consumed.

examples.explanation: $2\text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{MnO}_2} 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{O}_2$. $\text{MnO}_2$ written above arrow = catalyst. Speeds up decomposition without being consumed.

image.alt: Chemical equation showing catalyst above arrow
image.caption: Catalyst notation: written above arrow, not consumed in reaction.

examples.explanation: $\text{CaCO}_3(s) \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{CaO}(s) + \text{CO}_2(g)$. $\Delta$ (delta) above arrow = heat required. Reaction needs heat to occur (endothermic).

image.alt: Chemical equation showing heat symbol above arrow
image.caption: Heat notation: $\Delta$ above arrow indicates heat required.

principles.statement: Matter is neither created nor destroyed in chemical reactions; the total mass of reactants equals the total mass of products, and the number of each type of atom must be equal on both sides.
principles.application: Requires balancing chemical equations; atoms rearrange but don't disappear or appear. Mass of reactants = mass of products.
principles.example: $2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$. Left: 4 H, 2 O. Right: 4 H, 2 O. Atoms balanced, mass conserved. Unbalanced: $\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$ (2 O left, 1 O right = violates law).
principles.analogy: Like LEGO bricks: you can rearrange bricks into different structures, but you can't create or destroy bricks. Same atoms, different arrangements.

image.alt: Diagram showing atoms conserved in chemical reaction
image.caption: Conservation of mass: same atoms before and after, just rearranged.

principles.statement: A balanced chemical equation has equal numbers of each type of atom on both sides; only coefficients can be changed, never subscripts.
principles.application: Balancing ensures Law of Conservation of Mass is satisfied; coefficients adjusted to balance atoms; subscripts define substance identity and cannot change.
principles.example: Unbalanced: $\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$ (2 O left, 1 O right). Balanced: $2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ (4 H, 2 O on both sides). Only coefficients changed.

image.alt: Comparison of unbalanced and balanced equations
image.caption: Balanced equation: equal atoms on both sides; only coefficients change.

components.interpretation: Coefficients indicate relative amounts; subscripts indicate atoms per molecule; state symbols show physical state; arrow shows direction.

worked_examples.title: Reading Chemical Equations
worked_examples.problem: Interpret the following chemical equation: $2\text{Al}(s) + 3\text{CuCl}_2(aq) \rightarrow 2\text{AlCl}_3(aq) + 3\text{Cu}(s)$. Identify: (a) reactants, (b) products, (c) states of matter, (d) verify it is balanced.

step_by_step.calculation: Reactants (left side): $2\text{Al}(s)$ and $3\text{CuCl}_2(aq)$. Aluminum metal and copper(II) chloride solution.
Products (right side): $2\text{AlCl}_3(aq)$ and $3\text{Cu}(s)$. Aluminum chloride solution and copper metal.
(s) = solid: Al, Cu. (aq) = aqueous: $\text{CuCl}_2$, $\text{AlCl}_3$. Solid metals react in aqueous solution.
Left: 2 Al, 3 Cu, 6 Cl (3 × 2). Right: 2 Al, 3 Cu, 6 Cl (2 × 3). All atoms balanced: 2 Al, 3 Cu, 6 Cl on both sides. Equation is balanced.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Reactants: $\text{Al}(s)$ and $\text{CuCl}_2(aq)$. (b) Products: $\text{AlCl}_3(aq)$ and $\text{Cu}(s)$. (c) States: (s) = solid, (aq) = aqueous solution. (d) Balanced: 2 Al, 3 Cu, 6 Cl on both sides. This is a single replacement reaction where aluminum replaces copper.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Reading and interpreting chemical equation components

worked_examples.title: Distinguishing Coefficients from Subscripts
worked_examples.problem: For $3\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$, determine: (a) how many molecules, (b) how many H atoms total, (c) how many S atoms total, (d) how many O atoms total. Explain the difference between the coefficient and subscripts.

step_by_step.calculation: Coefficient 3 = 3 molecules of $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$
Subscript 2 means 2 H per molecule. Total H = 3 molecules × 2 H = 6 H atoms
No subscript means 1 S per molecule. Total S = 3 molecules × 1 S = 3 S atoms
Subscript 4 means 4 O per molecule. Total O = 3 molecules × 4 O = 12 O atoms

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) 3 molecules. (b) 6 H atoms (3 × 2). (c) 3 S atoms (3 × 1). (d) 12 O atoms (3 × 4). Coefficient (3) multiplies entire formula; subscripts (2, 4) indicate atoms per molecule. Coefficient can change when balancing; subscripts cannot change (defines substance identity).
worked_examples.concept_applied: Understanding coefficient vs. subscript in chemical formulas

sections.introduction: Chemical reactions can be classified into different types based on patterns of how reactants transform into products. Recognizing these patterns helps predict products, write balanced equations, and understand reaction mechanisms. Five major types cover most common reactions.

key_concept.definition: The five major types of chemical reactions are: synthesis (combination), decomposition, single replacement, double replacement, and combustion. Each type follows a characteristic pattern that helps predict products.
key_concept.context: Classifying reactions by type helps organize chemical knowledge and predict products. Some reactions fit multiple categories; some don't fit neatly into any category. These five types cover most common reactions encountered in introductory chemistry.

classifications.value: Two or more substances combine to form one product
classifications.behavior: Simpler substances combine to form more complex substance. Common with elements reacting to form compounds. Often releases energy.

examples.explanation: $2\text{Na}(s) + \text{Cl}_2(g) \rightarrow 2\text{NaCl}(s)$. Two elements combine to form ionic compound. Sodium metal + chlorine gas $\rightarrow$ table salt. Highly exothermic.

image.alt: Sodium and chlorine reacting to form sodium chloride
image.caption: Synthesis: $2\text{Na} + \text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{NaCl}$. Two elements form compound.

examples.explanation: $\text{CaO}(s) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l) \rightarrow \text{Ca}(\text{OH})_2(s)$. Two compounds combine. Calcium oxide + water $\rightarrow$ calcium hydroxide. Used in cement.

image.alt: Calcium oxide reacting with water
image.caption: Synthesis: $\text{CaO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Ca}(\text{OH})_2$. Two compounds form one.

classifications.value: One substance breaks down into two or more simpler substances
classifications.behavior: Complex substance breaks into simpler substances. Often requires heat, light, or electricity. Opposite pattern of synthesis reactions.

examples.explanation: $2\text{HgO}(s) \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2\text{Hg}(l) + \text{O}_2(g)$. Heat causes mercury(II) oxide to decompose. One compound $\rightarrow$ element + element. Requires heat input.

image.alt: Mercury oxide decomposing with heat
image.caption: Decomposition: $2\text{HgO} \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2\text{Hg} + \text{O}_2$. Heat required.

examples.explanation: $2\text{H}_2\text{O}(l) \xrightarrow{\text{electricity}} 2\text{H}_2(g) + \text{O}_2(g)$. Electricity decomposes water. One compound $\rightarrow$ two elements. Requires electrical energy.

image.alt: Water electrolysis producing hydrogen and oxygen
image.caption: Decomposition: $2\text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{electricity}} 2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2$. Electricity required.

classifications.value: One element replaces another element in a compound
characteristics: Element + compound $\rightarrow$ new element + new compound
classifications.behavior: More reactive element replaces less reactive element in compound. Activity series predicts which metals can replace others. Only occurs if replacing element is more reactive.

examples.explanation: $\text{Zn}(s) + \text{CuSO}_4(aq) \rightarrow \text{ZnSO}_4(aq) + \text{Cu}(s)$. Zinc replaces copper. Zinc more reactive than copper. Copper metal precipitates from solution.

image.alt: Zinc replacing copper in solution
image.caption: Single replacement: $\text{Zn} + \text{CuSO}_4 \rightarrow \text{ZnSO}_4 + \text{Cu}$. Zn replaces Cu.

examples.explanation: $\text{Mg}(s) + 2\text{HCl}(aq) \rightarrow \text{MgCl}_2(aq) + \text{H}_2(g)$. Magnesium replaces hydrogen in acid. Hydrogen gas bubbles form. Active metals react with acids.

image.alt: Magnesium reacting with acid producing hydrogen
image.caption: Single replacement: $\text{Mg} + 2\text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{MgCl}_2 + \text{H}_2$. Metal replaces H.

classifications.value: Two compounds exchange ions to form two new compounds
characteristics: Often forms precipitate, gas, or water
classifications.behavior: Positive ions (cations) and negative ions (anions) switch partners. Often occurs in aqueous solution. Reaction occurs if product is precipitate, gas, or water.

examples.explanation: $\text{AgNO}_3(aq) + \text{NaCl}(aq) \rightarrow \text{AgCl}(s)\downarrow + \text{NaNO}_3(aq)$. Ions exchange: $\text{Ag}^+$ pairs with $\text{Cl}^-$, $\text{Na}^+$ pairs with $\text{NO}_3^-$. Solid $\text{AgCl}$ precipitates.

image.alt: Double replacement forming precipitate
image.caption: Double replacement: $\text{AgNO}_3 + \text{NaCl} \rightarrow \text{AgCl}\downarrow + \text{NaNO}_3$. Precipitate forms.

examples.explanation: $\text{HCl}(aq) + \text{NaOH}(aq) \rightarrow \text{NaCl}(aq) + \text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$. Acid + base $\rightarrow$ salt + water. $\text{H}^+$ from acid pairs with $\text{OH}^-$ from base to form water.

image.alt: Acid-base neutralization reaction
image.caption: Neutralization: $\text{HCl} + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{NaCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$. Special double replacement.

classifications.value: Substance reacts rapidly with oxygen, releasing energy
classifications.behavior: Rapid oxidation releases energy as heat and light. Hydrocarbon combustion always produces $\text{CO}_2$ and $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ when complete. Incomplete combustion produces CO or C.

examples.explanation: $\text{CH}_4(g) + 2\text{O}_2(g) \rightarrow \text{CO}_2(g) + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(g) + \text{energy}$. Methane burns in oxygen. Produces $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, heat, and light. Natural gas combustion.

image.alt: Methane combustion flame
image.caption: Combustion: $\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{energy}$. Heat and light released.

examples.explanation: $2\text{Mg}(s) + \text{O}_2(g) \rightarrow 2\text{MgO}(s) + \text{energy}$. Magnesium burns in oxygen. Produces bright white light and heat. Metal + oxygen $\rightarrow$ metal oxide.

image.alt: Magnesium ribbon burning brightly
image.caption: Combustion: $2\text{Mg} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{MgO} + \text{energy}$. Bright white light.

principles.statement: In single replacement reactions, a more reactive element can replace a less reactive element in a compound; the activity series ranks elements by reactivity.
principles.application: Predicts if single replacement will occur: Zn can replace Cu (Zn more reactive), but Cu cannot replace Zn (Cu less reactive). Activity series: Li > K > Ca > Na > Mg > Al > Zn > Fe > ... > Cu > Ag > Au.
principles.example: $\text{Zn} + \text{CuSO}_4 \rightarrow \text{ZnSO}_4 + \text{Cu}$ occurs (Zn > Cu in activity series). $\text{Cu} + \text{ZnSO}_4 \rightarrow$ no reaction (Cu < Zn, cannot replace).

image.alt: Activity series chart showing metal reactivity order
image.caption: Activity series: more reactive metals can replace less reactive metals.

principles.statement: Double replacement reactions occur when one product is insoluble (precipitate), a gas, or water; solubility rules predict which ionic compounds are soluble in water.
principles.application: Predicts if double replacement will occur: if both products are soluble, no reaction. Common soluble: all nitrates, all Group 1 salts. Common insoluble: most carbonates, most hydroxides (except Group 1).
principles.example: $\text{AgNO}_3 + \text{NaCl} \rightarrow \text{AgCl}\downarrow + \text{NaNO}_3$ occurs ($\text{AgCl}$ insoluble). $\text{NaNO}_3 + \text{KCl} \rightarrow$ no reaction (all products soluble).

image.alt: Solubility rules chart for ionic compounds
image.caption: Solubility rules: predict which ionic compounds form precipitates.

components.equation: $\begin{cases} \text{Synthesis: } A + B \rightarrow AB \\ \text{Decomposition: } AB \rightarrow A + B \\ \text{Single Replacement: } A + BC \rightarrow AC + B \\ \text{Double Replacement: } AB + CD \rightarrow AD + CB \\ \text{Combustion: } \text{Fuel} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Oxides} + \text{energy} \end{cases}$
components.interpretation: Each type has characteristic pattern; recognizing pattern helps predict products and classify reactions. Some reactions fit multiple categories.

worked_examples.title: Classifying Chemical Reactions by Type

step_by_step.calculation: $2\text{KClO}_3 \rightarrow 2\text{KCl} + 3\text{O}_2$. One reactant $\rightarrow$ multiple products. Pattern: $AB \rightarrow A + B$. Type: Decomposition.
$\text{Ca} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Ca}(\text{OH})_2 + \text{H}_2$. Element + compound $\rightarrow$ new element + new compound. Ca replaces H. Type: Single Replacement.
$\text{N}_2 + 3\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{NH}_3$. Multiple reactants $\rightarrow$ one product. Pattern: $A + B \rightarrow AB$. Type: Synthesis.
$\text{C}_3\text{H}_8 + 5\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 3\text{CO}_2 + 4\text{H}_2\text{O}$. Hydrocarbon + $\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{energy}$. Type: Combustion.
$\text{NaOH} + \text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{NaCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$. Two compounds $\rightarrow$ two new compounds. Ions exchange. Type: Double Replacement (neutralization).

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Decomposition (one $\rightarrow$ multiple). (b) Single Replacement (element replaces element in compound). (c) Synthesis (multiple $\rightarrow$ one). (d) Combustion (hydrocarbon + $\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$). (e) Double Replacement (ions exchange, acid-base neutralization). Recognizing patterns enables classification.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Classifying chemical reactions by type based on reactant/product patterns

worked_examples.title: Predicting Products and Balancing Reactions

step_by_step.calculation: Type: Synthesis (element + element). Product: $\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ (Al³⁺, O²⁻). Unbalanced: $\text{Al} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$. Balanced: $4\text{Al} + 3\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$.

Type: Single Replacement (metal + acid). Mg replaces H. Products: $\text{MgCl}_2 + \text{H}_2$. Unbalanced: $\text{Mg} + \text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{MgCl}_2 + \text{H}_2$. Balanced: $\text{Mg} + 2\text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{MgCl}_2 + \text{H}_2$.

Type: Double Replacement (two ionic compounds). Ions exchange: $\text{Na}^+$ with $\text{Cl}^-$, $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ with $\text{CO}_3^{2-}$. Products: $\text{NaCl} + \text{CaCO}_3$. $\text{CaCO}_3$ is insoluble (precipitate). Balanced: $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{CaCl}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{NaCl} + \text{CaCO}_3\downarrow$.

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) $4\text{Al} + 3\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$ (synthesis). (b) $\text{Mg} + 2\text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{MgCl}_2 + \text{H}_2$ (single replacement). (c) $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{CaCl}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{NaCl} + \text{CaCO}_3\downarrow$ (double replacement, precipitate). Pattern recognition enables product prediction; then balance atoms.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Predicting products from reaction type and balancing equations

summary: Chemical reactions involve rearrangement of atoms through bond breaking and forming, producing new substances with different properties. Evidence includes: color change, gas production, precipitate formation, temperature change, and light emission. Chemical equations represent reactions: reactants $\rightarrow$ products, with coefficients indicating relative amounts. Law of Conservation of Mass requires balanced equations (equal atoms on both sides). Five major reaction types: (1) Synthesis: $A + B \rightarrow AB$ (multiple $\rightarrow$ one), (2) Decomposition: $AB \rightarrow A + B$ (one $\rightarrow$ multiple), (3) Single Replacement: $A + BC \rightarrow AC + B$ (element replaces element), (4) Double Replacement: $AB + CD \rightarrow AD + CB$ (ions exchange), (5) Combustion: fuel + $\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{oxides} + \text{energy}$ (rapid oxidation). Activity series predicts single replacement; solubility rules predict double replacement. State symbols: (s) solid, (l) liquid, (g) gas, (aq) aqueous. Coefficients can change when balancing; subscripts cannot (defines substance). Physical changes alter form without changing composition; chemical changes produce new substances. Understanding reaction types enables product prediction and equation balancing.
